system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — chord-chemistry

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This guitar method book proceeds in three broad phases. The first phase (Chapters 1–8) builds the complete theoretical and referential infrastructure: fingerboard geography and notation conventions (Chapters 1–2), right-hand technique taxonomy (Chapter 3), scale and interval theory (Chapter 4), chord formulas and families (Chapters 5–6), and comprehensive chord synonym and reference charts (Chapters 7–8). The second phase (Chapters 9–16) moves from reference to application, developing ear training (Chapter 9), harmonic motion through the cycle of 4ths (Chapters 10–12), voice leading as a systematic discipline (Chapter 13), and the practical forms of chord melody, triads, and diatonic scale harmonies (Chapters 14–16). The third phase (Chapters 17–20) applies all prior systems to idiomatic contexts — modal and non-diatonic scales (Chapter 17), blues (Chapters 18–19), and rock (Chapter 20). Throughout, the book consistently subordinates mechanical pattern-learning to formula-based reasoning: students are taught *why* a chord or substitution works before they are expected to play it.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The book's organizing system is the **chord formula**, defined in Chapter 4 as a combination of scale-degree numbers drawn from the major scale built on the same root. Three foundational **chord families** — MAJOR, MINOR, and DOMINANT 7TH — are established in Chapter 5 as the parents from which all other chords derive. **Extensions** (9th, 11th, 13th, treated as compound intervals interchangeable with 2nd, 4th, 6th per Chapters 5–6), **altered chords** (containing ♯5, ♭5, ♭9, or ♯9), and **suspended chords** (3rd replaced by 4th) complete the taxonomic vocabulary. The book introduces the **same-string inversion method** in Chapters 4–5 as its signature voicing tool: raising each chord tone on its string to the next higher tone in the chord, which produces exactly N distinct forms for any N-note chord on a fixed string set. Chapter 2 establishes two sets of neck **reference points** (5th fret and 12th fret) and the 1st/6th string symmetry rule that halves fingerboard memorization. Chapter 6 codifies **polytonal chord naming** — parsing a symbol like C7♭9♭5 mechanically to its formula (1, 3, ♭5, ♭7, ♭9) — so that no chord requires rote lookup. The **cycle of 4ths/5ths** (Chapter 12) is the macro-structural backbone, with dominant 7th chords generating harmonic momentum through cyclical motion.

## Rules and Prescriptions

The book establishes a dense set of explicit rules. On voicing: altered 5ths must be retained in any voicing (Chapter 5); roots or 5ths may be omitted in five-note chords; 3rds are frequently omitted in 11th chords; the bottom triad of a polychord may omit its 5th (Chapter 7); when playing with a bassist, upper voicings must be kept high to avoid muddiness (Chapter 7). On substitution: the **tritone substitution** principle (Chapters 11–12) establishes that dominants a 5th apart are functionally related; altered minors are restricted to m7♭5 (Chapter 11); m6 chords must not be used where they conflict with the following dominant a 4th higher (Chapter 11); augmented chords repeat symmetrically every 4 frets and diminished 7th chords every 3 frets — both usable as passing chords (Chapters 8, 11). On blues and turnarounds: turnarounds must cadence on V7 or its ♭5 voicing (Chapter 19); in fragment substitutions, the ending chord matters more than the starting chord (Chapter 19). On altered dominants: altered I7 chords should not open a song, except 7♯9 (Chapter 10); the 11♭9 chord is reserved for authentic V7 function (Chapter 12). On chord melody: the melody must always sit above the chord voicing (Chapter 14); chords are typically played on the first note of each measure rather than every note (Chapter 14). On technique: certain chord voicings demand finger technique rather than flat pick alone, with specific string-count rules governing pick-plus-fingers assignments (Chapter 3).

## Explicit Methodology and Pedagogical Moves

The book's explicit methodology is **systematic formula-based thinking**. Chapter 13 names it directly: break every chord into its individual tones, treat each tone as a separate voice, and analyze how each voice moves to the next chord. This "systematic thinking" is asserted as the reason every chord in the reference charts earns its place, and as the mechanism that makes any chord usable when properly voice-led. Chapter 6 applies the same logic to chord construction: students parse symbols mechanically rather than memorizing isolated shapes. Chapter 7 counters the temptation to accumulate chords without application, prescribing that memorization be organized by **physical proximity on the fingerboard** rather than by arbitrary collection, and insisting that knowing many chords without knowing where to use them is senseless.

**Back-cycling** (introduced in Chapters 14–15) is a recurring compositional tool: preceding any chord with its own V7 creates voice-leading momentum, and the two-chord back-cycling rule (moving back two chords yields V and V-of-V) extends this systematically. **Voice leading** is formalized in Chapter 13 with V7–I as the primary laboratory, establishing leading-tone resolution (the 3rd of V7 moving up to the root of I) and permitting 5th omission when voice leading is strong. Chapter 14 introduces the m(maj7)–m7 voice-leading rule (the 7 tone moving one fret up or down into a root or 6/7 tone) as a concrete application of these principles.

**Ear training** (Chapter 9) is structured as categorical triage: any heard chord is first classified into one of three aural categories — major, minor, or dominant — before finer identification. The chapter balances mental categorization with the explicit prescription that experience is the primary teacher for progression recognition. This empirical-plus-analytical balance recurs throughout: Chapter 10 frames substitution guidelines as non-rigid rules that competent improvisers adapt in real time, and Chapter 17 frames modal harmony in terms of **tonal center** thinking rather than exhaustive scale-chord labeling, explicitly warning against "dry" or "stiff" execution.

The book's modal vocabulary (Chapter 17) establishes **scale equivalence** — C major = G mixolydian = A natural minor = D dorian minor — as the conceptual bridge between key-based and mode-based thinking. Blues (Chapters 18–19) is defined structurally by the 12-bar form with I in bar 1 and IV in bar 5, harmonically dominated by the dominant 7th family, and extensible through turnarounds, chromatic voice-leading (stepwise motion without skipping), and reinterpreted IV minor chords. Rock (Chapter 20) adds the characteristic transformation of lowering the 3rd and ♭7th of a dominant by one fret to generate IV9 chords, the resolving diminished technique, and the I–IV–I sandwich pattern — all presented as formula-derivable rather than memorized idioms, consistent with the book's overarching commitment to systematic harmonic reasoning over pattern accumulation.

# Chapter 2 — Chord Reference Charts Explanation

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 provides foundational reference material for navigating the guitar neck and reading chord diagrams. It introduces the core neck-memorization system built on two sets of reference points—the 1st reference points at the 5th fret (4th fret for the 3rd string) that match the open strings one octave higher, and the 2nd reference points at the 12th fret that duplicate the open strings an octave up—along with the 1st and 6th string symmetry rule that halves memorization load. The chapter establishes essential terminology and conventions: open string, enharmonic equivalence (Gb and F#), moveable chords, and the silent/deadened strings rule for chord voicing. It also codifies the book's notation system, including finger numbering, the 1st finger split-fret technique, and chord symbol conventions (slash for major type, + for sharpened 5th, sus for 4th replacing 3rd). The chapter prescribes a systematic 15-minute daily practice to internalize the fingerboard and defines the term 'TYPE' to categorize related chord families.

# Chapter 3 — Right Hand Technique

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 introduces five foundational right-hand techniques for guitar: the flat pick, thumb pick with fingers, flat pick with fingers, fingers and thumb with no pick, and thumb only. The chapter establishes that players need not restrict themselves to a single technique, as many accomplished guitarists employ different approaches for different sounds. Chet Atkins exemplifies the thumb-pick-with-fingers approach. The load-bearing constraint is that certain chords demand finger-based techniques for playability; specifically, 4-note chords in the flat-pick-with-fingers style require the pick on the bass note and one finger per remaining string with the little finger taking the highest note, 5- and 6-note chords require brushing (one or two fingers covering multiple strings), and 3-note chords use only 2 fingers plus pick or 3 fingers alone. This taxonomy of techniques and their chord-specific rules forms the foundation for all subsequent right-hand instruction.

# Chapter 4 — The Major Scale

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 introduces the foundational vocabulary and construction rules for understanding scales and chords. The chapter defines scale (synonymous with mode) as a group of notes with intervals between them, then establishes two core intervals: whole step (2 frets apart) and half step (adjacent frets). The major scale follows a characteristic interval formula of W-W-H-W-W-W-H from any starting note (the root or tonic), with notes numbered 1–8 in a scale-degree system used for chord formulas and interval naming (inclusive counting). The chapter introduces Roman numeral notation for naming chords within a major scale key, then explains that every chord has a formula—a specific combination of scale tones—distinguishing triads (3-tone chords) from extensions (added tones like 7ths), altered chords (with raised or lowered 5ths or 9ths), and suspended chords (3rd replaced by 4th). Finally, the chapter defines inversions or voicings as different arrangements of chord tones, with the book's characteristic method for generating them: raising each tone on the same string to the next higher tone in the chord.

# Chapter 5 — Chord Formulas and Families

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 establishes the foundational taxonomy and mechanics of chord construction on guitar. The chapter teaches that chord formulas are built relative to the major scale with the same root, and introduces three structural categories: FORMULA (the tones used to build a chord), TRIAD (a three-tone chord), and three chord families—MAJOR, MINOR, and DOMINANT 7TH—from which all other chords derive. Key extensions and modifications include EXTENSIONS (tones added to basic chords), ALTERED chords (containing a #5th, b5th, b9th, or #9th), and SUSPENDED chords (where the 3rd is replaced by a 4th). The master's characteristic moves center on voicing discipline: the same-string inversion method (raising each tone on its string to the next higher tone in the chord) yields a memorable rule that any N-note chord has exactly N different forms on the same set of strings, along with practical guidelines for omitting or doubling tones — altered 5ths must be retained, roots or 5ths may be omitted in five-note chords, and 3rds are often omitted in 11th chords. The chapter also clarifies load-bearing terminology including INVERSIONS or VOICINGS, the compound interval naming convention (9th/11th/13th from piano theory), and voicing rules for altered extensions like placing #9 above the 3rd and +11 above the 5th.

# Chapter 7 — Essential Chords, Synonyms

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

The chapter introduces polychords as an alternative analytical viewpoint for understanding chord construction, defining them as two superimposed triads (with the practical voicing rule that the bottom triad may omit its 5th). Polychord thinking is positioned for ensemble contexts — particularly when playing with bass players or other harmonic contributors — where a guitarist can superimpose upper triads over a bassist's root note, keeping those upper voicings high to avoid muddiness. The chapter establishes that the "essential chords" (major, minor, major 6th, minor 6th, major 7th, minor 7th, dominant 7th, and extensions up to 13th and 11th) are sufficient for nearly any musical situation and prescribes a learning order progressing from simple to complex. A core pedagogical principle emerges: knowing many chords without knowing where to apply them is senseless, and memorization is accelerated by grouping physically related chords together on the fingerboard rather than collecting voicings arbitrarily. The master's characteristic move here is pragmatic realism — framing theory (polychords) as useful only within ensemble application and prioritizing functional application over exhaustive harmonic knowledge.

# Chapter 8 — Chord Reference Charts

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 presents a comprehensive reference guide to chord synonyms and their practical application in substitution. The chapter establishes that chords with different names but identical notes can be used interchangeably, with the primary benefit being the discovery of substitute chords through synonym knowledge. Key rules introduced include the add9+ ≡ 7+ equivalence (eliminating add9+ from the charts), the 9-suspended derivation (9-sus as a 9-chord without the 3rd), and specific omission rules for altered dominants like 13b9. The dominant family receives disproportionate coverage due to containing more chord types than major or minor families. The chapter also addresses augmented chords, which exhibit symmetry and repeat every 4 frets, distinguishing between +11 and 9b5 dominant chords by tone content, and noting that 9b5 chords are preferred over 9#5b5 for their clearer sound and usability, with the exceptional 9#5b5 having only one playable inversion.

# Chapter 9 — Ear Training

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 teaches ear training through a structured, three-stage progression: first, distinguish major and minor triads by their characteristic sound; second, locate dominant 7th chords relative to the major/minor poles, and recognize how major/major-7 and minor/minor-7 chords form closely related families; third, practice hearing chord progressions through experience and familiarity. The master's approach is categorical and triage-focused — when hearing a chord, immediately classify it into one of three aural categories (major, minor, or dominant), excluding only diminished, augmented, and chords without thirds. The chapter introduces the term "implied chords" to describe harmony that arises from two voices or melodies without explicit chord voicings, and emphasizes that progression-recognition comes mainly through experience. The method synthesizes both mental categorization of sounds and cumulative listening experience: experience is the best teacher, but a thorough study and mental categorization of sounds is essential to understanding and hearing chords and chord progressions.

# Chapter 10 — Moving Chords a 4th

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 teaches chord substitution techniques centered on the move-by-4th principle and altered dominants. The master's characteristic moves include partial duration-splitting substitutions (playing a ii-V m7 type chord for part of a dominant's duration, then resolving to the dominant), tritone-sub interpolations that inject a dominant a half-step above before resolution to a chord a 4th higher, and the V-of-V dominant substitution where a dominant's root moves up a 5th. Key terminology includes altered dominant contexts (sounding #5th, b5th, #9th, or combinations), the 7#9 chord as a bluesy direct substitution, and the principle that the top note (soprano voice) is critical in altered voicings. The chapter emphasizes that these are guidelines, not rigid rules, and that altered I7 chords should not open a song (except 7#9); competent improvisers adapt to substitutions heard in real time. The closing directive encourages experimentation with the move-by-4th principle to develop intuition for the guitar's nature.

# Chapter 11 — Chord Substitutions and General Information

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 covers chord substitutions and harmonic devices for expanding comping vocabulary and smoothing voice-leading. The master establishes that dominant chords a 5th apart are related (tritone-substitution principle), restricts altered minors to m7b5, and demonstrates that m6 chords should avoid contexts where they conflict with the following dominant a 4th higher. Key substitutions include dominant 11th chords (especially with no 3rd) and augmented 11th chords as altered-dominant replacements, though +11 cannot function as tonic; diminished 7th chords repeat enharmonically every 3 frets and serve as passing chords between other harmonies. The chapter teaches practical techniques like using common tones to smooth resolutions, employing dominant sus chords as partial or complete replacements for dominant 7ths, omitting the 3rd in major chords for sus-flavored textures, and deploying major 7th and major 9th voicings as passing chords. The load-bearing concepts are tritone substitution, altered dominants (particularly the +11 and 9b5), voice-leading through common tones and passing chords, and the enharmonic symmetry of diminished 7th chords.

# Chapter 12 — Dominant 7th Chords and the Cycle

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch12.md`._

This chapter teaches how dominant 7th chords and their alterations work within the cycle of 4ths/5ths, the organizing principle behind common-practice progressions. The master introduces augmented chords as dominant-derived (7#5 with no b7), establishes motion rules for dominants — 3-fret intervals for standard types, 2-fret intervals for altered dominants with 5ths and/or #5ths — and presents the m(maj7) chord as a half-step substitution for V7 function. Key techniques include the ii-V-I progression for smooth modulation to new keys, the 11b9 chord reserved for authentic V7 function, and the use of common tones in upper voices to ease transitions. Load-bearing terminology includes cycle of 4ths (also 5ths), V7 function (dominants as secondary dominants), modulation, and cycle-rooted chords in series, which form the framework for understanding how dominant chords create harmonic momentum and key changes in popular music.

# Chapter 13 — Moving Voices (Voice Leading) and Systematic Thinking

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Chapter 13 teaches voice leading — the principle of treating each note of a chord separately as it moves to the next chord — as the fundamental technique for smooth harmonic progressions and modern chord usage. The chapter centers on V7 to I as the foundational voice-leading laboratory, establishing rules such as leading-tone resolution (the 3rd of V7 moving up to the root of I) and permitting structural liberties like 5th omission when voice leading is strong; it further demonstrates how voice leading justifies chromatic approach chords, anticipations, and the ii-V cliché (a dominant chord a 4th above a minor 7th). The master's characteristic move is systematic thinking — breaking down each chord into its tones and analyzing each voice separately — which the chapter argues is the reason every chord in the reference charts earns its place and why any chord becomes usable when properly voice-led. Key terminology introduced includes voice (each note of a chord), voice leading (how a voice moves chord to chord), and transformations like the major 7th rule (lowering one root in a two-root major chord), with special attention to m(maj7) as a chord type that necessitates voice-leading principles.

# Chapter 14 — Chord Melodies

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch14.md`._

Chapter 14 introduces chord melody, the technique of playing a song's melody and chords simultaneously, with the melody always positioned above the chord voicing. The chapter establishes foundational rules: the melody must be raised an octave, should correspond to a tone within the underlying chord or its extensions, and chords are typically played on the first note of each measure rather than on every note. Key enhancements include using relative majors and minors (chords sharing the same key signature) by analyzing the top-note relationship between melody and chord, employing the Picardy-third technique (ending a minor song on a major chord), and back-cycling (preceding each chord with its V chord to create voice-leading movement). The m(maj7)–m7 voice-leading rule specifies that the 7 tone should move up or down one fret into a root or 6/7 tone when transitioning between these chord types.

# Chapter 15 — Triads

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch15.md`._

Chapter 15 introduces triads — any chord containing three notes — and focuses on their close voicing, where tones are arranged as closely as possible on the guitar, with only 12 different forms of close-voiced major triads available for any key. The chapter establishes I-IV as the foundational triad sound requiring immediate memorization across the entire neck. Key techniques include reinterpreting ii-I progressions as ii-V-I for back-cycling opportunities, applying the back-cycling two-chord rule (moving back 2 chords yields the V and V of V), and using chromatic-up and tritone substitutions within back-cycles for harmonic variation. For fast melodies or alternate sounds, interval harmonization below the melody using 3rd, 6th, 10th, 13th, or 17th intervals offers a solution, and the chapter recommends studying piano arrangements for cross-instrument harmonic ideas applicable to guitar.

# Chapter 16 — Fundamental Harmonies of Scales

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch16.md`._

Chapter 16 teaches the fundamental harmonies built from scales, establishing that every scale contains inherent diatonic chords derived through the next-chord-up construction — moving each tone in a chord up to the next scale tone to build the sequence. The master's characteristic moves in this section include voicing rules for practical application: using open triads with root-position bass at phrase endings, doubling triad tones (root, 3rd, or 5th) to create four-note chords, and omitting the root or 5th when reducing seventh chords to triad form for harmonization. Load-bearing terminology includes open voicing (any arrangement other than closed), diatonic seventh-chord sequences (I, ii–m7, iii–m7, IV, V7, vi–m7, vii°), and the critical insight that vii° (diminished triad) functions as a rootless V7 — a substitution principle for modulation. The chapter frames these techniques in the context of practical applications like Christmas carol harmonization and key transitions, where V7 or V always sets up modulation to a new key.

# Chapter 17 — Other Chords Built from Scales

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch17.md`._

Chapter 17 extends harmonic understanding by teaching that chords can be derived from any scale — not just the fundamental diatonic chords — provided their notes belong to that scale, and that any such chord can serve as the seed for building a chord scale using the same diatonic-step procedures. Key load-bearing terms introduced are tonal center (the scale-based key context, synonymous with key) and modal scale equivalence (the principle that C major = G mixolydian = A natural minor = D dorian minor, underpinning modal harmony). The chapter establishes stylistic conventions: natural minor progressions favor 3-tone chords (triads) over 4-tone extensions for their ease in modulation, while mixolydian harmonies lend themselves to blues and rock idioms; practical substitutions include using a plain minor chord in place of Im(maj7) in harmonic minor contexts. Rather than labeling every chord in these scales, working musicians think of them in terms of their tonal center, and expressive playing — avoiding "dry" or "stiff" execution — is essential to making these progressions sound musical.

# Chapter 18 — Blues Progressions

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch18.md`._

Chapter 18 teaches blues progressions as a fundamental form essential to modern music, defining blues within this book as any 12-bar progression anchored structurally by a I chord in bar 1 and a IV chord in bar 5. The master's characteristic move emphasizes the dominance of the dominant 7th family as the defining harmonic color of blues, with 9th chords providing additional bluesy character through extension richness. Core terminology includes the 12-bar form, dominant 7th, voice leading (referenced to section 13), and chord inversions, all selected with top-note attention to maintain smooth melodic lines. The chapter reinforces that right-hand technique matters — some progressions require fingers rather than pick alone — and anchors its approach in the understand-why principle: do what sounds good, but ground it in harmonic analysis and accepted blues practice.

# Chapter 19 — Miscellaneous Concepts

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch19.md`._

Chapter 19 introduces foundational chord-substitution and turnaround concepts that extend the 12-bar blues framework. The chapter defines turnaround as any chord series replacing extended tonic duration, establishes that turnarounds must cadence on V7 or its b5 voicing, and shows how #IV° functions as a passing chord within bar 6. Key reinterpretation moves include treating IVm as ii of a new dominant and substituting IIIm7/III7 in measure 7, while the turnaround technique can anchor introductory passages. The master's characteristic move is recognizing harmonic flexibility through substitution: IVm reinterpreted, scale-fragment harmonies deployed, and chromatic voice-leading employed (defined as stepwise motion without skipping). Load-bearing terminology includes turnaround, chromatic motion, scale-fragment substitution, and the distinction between major blues and minor blues forms. A critical rule emerges for fragment substitution: the starting chord matters less than the ending chord, which must align with the harmony being replaced.

# Chapter 20 — Rock Type Progressions

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch20.md`._

Chapter 20 teaches voice-leading transformations and substitution techniques specific to rock harmony. The master's characteristic moves include lowering the 3rd and b7th of dominant chords by one fret to generate IV9 chords — a transformation that generalizes across any dominant and can be applied progressively through cycle-based dominant series — and using only the 3rd and b7 shell voicing as a melodic foundation. Two additional techniques define the chapter: the resolving diminished chord (constructed from any three notes of a diminished 7th chord one fret above the V7's root, resolving to I or m(maj7)) and the I-IV-I sandwich pattern, where the IV chord sits between two I chords, extended via cycle motion by inserting the IV-of-IV before the IV. The chapter also establishes the stylistic principle that major chords in rock can substitute dominant 7th chords and their extensions.

## Five right-hand techniques

> There are at least 5 different right hand techniques being used by guitarists:
> 1) The flat pick (commonly referred to as just the ‘‘pick’’).
> 2) The thumb pick and fingers — using the fingers of the right hand with a pick on the thumb only
> (a la Chet Atkins).
> 3) The flat pick and fingers — using the middle, ring, and little fingers with the pick.
> 4) The fingers and thumb with no pick.
> 5) The thumb only.

**Significance (p. 8).** Foundational taxonomy that the chapter is organized around.

## Technique selection: multiple styles

> You do not have to use
> only one technique, as many fine players use different ones for different sounds.

**Significance (p. 8).** Explicit rule permitting technique mixing.

## Finger techniques required for chords

> many of the chords in this book require the use of either one of the techniques using the
> fingers (2, 3, or 4 above), because they would not be playable any other way.

**Significance (p. 8).** Hard rule tying right-hand technique choice to chord playability.

## Flat pick + fingers: 4-note chords

> On 4 note chords, there is no big problem,
> once you get used to the technique; you just use the pick on the bass (lowest in pitch) note and one
> finger for each of the remaining 3 strings, the little finger getting the highest note.

**Significance (p. 8).** Specific finger assignment rule for technique 3 on 4-note chords.

## Flat pick + fingers: 5- and 6-note chords

> when a
> chord has 5 or 6 notes, one or two of the fingers must get 2 strings by brushing across the strings.

**Significance (p. 8).** Defines the brushing adaptation for fuller voicings.

## Flat pick + fingers: 3-note chords

> On 3
> note chords use only 2 fingers and the pick, or 3 fingers.

**Significance (p. 8).** Rule specifying reduced finger count for 3-note chords.

## Lowering 3rd and b7th yields IV9

> if you take any 7th chord and lower the 3rd and 7th 1 fret each, you will
> get a 9th chord built on the IV.

**Significance (p. 106).** States the dominant→IV9 voice-leading transformation rule.

## 3rd-b7th lowering general rule

> You actually can take the 3rd and 57th of ANY dominant chord, and by lowering them | fret each,
> you will get some form of a IV dominant chord

**Significance (p. 106).** Generalizes the lowering transformation to any dominant.

## Progressive lowering through cycle

> In any progression that has a series of dominant chords based on the cycle, you may progressively
> lower the 3rd and 57th, one fret at a time.

**Significance (p. 106).** Voice-leading rule through cycle-based dominant series.

## 3rd+b7 + melody as fragment

> you may just use the 3rd and b7th of any dominant chord and play melodies on top for an
> interesting effect.

**Significance (p. 106).** Defines the 3rd-b7-shell-voicing technique.

**Significance (p. 106).** Constructs the resolving diminished chord for V-I.

**Significance (p. 106).** Names the technique central to Section 19.

>
> also work well in place of m# type of chords, as long as the 7th tone of the m+is in the resolving dim.

> chord.
